text: My guest on this episode is Ramp's VP of Growth and Demand, George Banassi. Ramp is one of the fastest moving companies in tech right now, and a lot of people will tell you they're one of the best brands in B2B to follow when it comes to marketing. George runs growth at Ramp and reports into the CTO, which is interesting. We talk about their marketing org there. And he's living at the intersection of AI and marketing every day. A couple of things that we got into on this episode. George used to be the most hardcore attribution guy you've ever met. He was actually a chemist and a two-time founder in his past life before he was running growth at Ramp. And he believed if you couldn't measure it, he didn't want to hear about it. But now he's completely reversed his take on that. And he explains why anything measurable is now a race to the bottom. We talk about how Ramp is restructuring their marketing team and whole marketing motion around AI agents and what their internal tool Project Glass is actually doing for them every day. And we get into why Ramp is betting so hard also on the other side of the spectrum here on in-person events and stunts and how George thinks about attention as the real moat. Enjoy my conversation with George from Ramp. All right, so George is here. George is the VP of Growth at Ramp. And I didn't actually know this before we reached out to have you on, but you also worked at Gong, so you got some cool background. But give me the two-minute overview on George. How did you get to Ramp? People don't want to. I used to do this all the time. Like, tell me what you do. How did you get a degree in? What did you do? People are like, dude, just tell me how Ramp does marketing. They want, like, you show me cool stuff you do. But I do want to level set with your, like, background a little bit. So let's kind of, like, segue into becoming the VP, you know, getting the VP of growth role at Ramp. Sure. Well, kind of a nontraditional background. So I was originally, my first career was as an analytical chemist. So I studied biochemistry in college. Whoa. Dude, I've been doing this. I've talked to so many people in this space, and it's always like a nontraditional background. I was an engineer. I was a musician. You might be the first, what was it, organic chemist, would you say? So I studied biochemistry, but most of my work was in analytical chemistry. So I worked in an analytical lab for a while. I did research. I worked in a private lab. And then I had two startups in kind of the biotech space doing synthetic biology, one that was a very, very small success, the one that was a massive failure. We got shut down by the federal government, which is a story all on its own. Startups that you worked at or you started the company I started I was the co That so cool And so yeah yeah So after that got shut down I was like I should probably get out of the lab and learn something about business So that's how I ended up in tech, as you do. And I joined a 20-person startup making labs or scientific software for folks that work in labs. And then I showed up the first day. Like, I just applied cold. And I was like, what do you guys want me to do? And why'd you hire me? And they're like, yeah, we heard of your first company, so we figured you're a smart guy. Can you figure out email marketing? And that's kind of how I got into marketing. I just kind of stuck with it. Yeah, dude, I'm a scientist. I can absolutely figure out how to use HubSpot. We got this. It's funny because I talk to so many marketers, and so many marketers have this existential crisis. Like, is this who I am? Is this who I was put on this earth to be a B2B marketer? But then I also have – it's like grass is always greener or like every rapper wants to be an NBA and every NBA player wants to be a rapper. It's like you have all these other people from like really smart fields and other things and they all come into marketing and business. And so it's very interesting to hear this, to get that perspective. I mean it's like I always tell people that I get to know. It's like I don't really care about marketing at all. Like I'm not that interested. I just like solving problems. Sure. There's always a lot of problems. Solving the fun problems. Well, I think that actually plays into like how you guys do marketing and a bunch of stuff you've done at Ramp and even just in your background, which is like when you think of VP of growth, I think my brain defaults to like, you know, Sean Ellis growth hacking, like that era of SaaS. But when I see the stuff that Ramp does, it's very much like – it's almost like brand, but growth is – brand is the growth lever. And we kind of have this in our prep doc, but it's like a lot of the stuff you're doing on the growth side, it's not just tests with high-volume landing pages and ads. It's like, no, we're going to do this stunt with this guy in the box in Times Square. We're going to like, even just you guys sent me a box of swag and I'm like, all the stuff in the swag, all the stuff in the box was like stuff that I'm not going to throw out. It's actually good stuff. It's like, so anyway. Yeah, I mean, I honestly can't take any credit for that. Like most of the really creative stuff we do, like I, it's not my skill set. I don spike there And most of the time I tell there one very talented person on my team that leads a lot of our stunts and uh i remember like for the brian stunt where we put brian um who played kevin from the office in a box in new york i was like i think this is a terrible idea i don't think it's gonna work at all but you have a lot of convictions to go for it uh and she totally proved me wrong uh and i like to tell that story because i think that's like ramp to a t it is not top down it is bottoms up well but i think there's um there's also something that like the conviction of things, you know, and it's like how bad when, when someone on the team has a really strong opinion, cause you as a team leader, whatever role you play in the company, you can't just know everything and be everywhere. And so you have to have smart people. And when someone has a, even on our small team with X, if I'm like, all right, is this a hill that I should die on? Like, should I be the one to kill this? Or like, no, they really feel like they want to do that. I got, okay, I got to let it like, let's do it. Like I can't be the only smart person here. Like you're not. So, so let's level set and just what is the, what is the structure of the marketing? Like where does marketing fit at ramp? Can we, we, I have some topics in my doc that I want to go through, but if we could just kind of level set and like, I think as people listen to hearing about what you do there in growth and marketing, it's helpful just to see like what, how the org looks and where marketing fits in the org. Sure. Uh, it is a very non-traditional org, which is largely why, or one of the many reasons why I joined Ramp, honestly. So it's not a central function. And honestly, like most functions at Ramp are not very centralized. It's very, again, bottoms up. You form pods. You move quickly. And so marketing at Ramp is product marketing, which actually sits under the product org technically. It is brand, which sits under a VP of brand. It's comms, P-R-A-R, which actually reports up to the – a head of comms who reports to the CEO. And then it's me, which is growth, and I report to the CTO. So growth at Ramp is, I mean, it's the traditional stuff, paid search, paid social, like performance marketing, it's lifecycle, SEO. So that's interesting. So there's no like CM in a traditional, I don't even know traditional, many other companies there'd be a CMO and all those roles would feed into the CMO. It's like Ramp has split those up and product marketing is under product, growth is under CTO, and then some of the other more corporate marketing go to the CEO? Exactly. That's exactly right. Yeah. Is that good or bad? Do you have any opinions on that? I think it's been phenomenal. I honestly feel bad for some CMOs just because I think it's in a lot of ways an impossible job. The breadth of what you have to own and be good at is very diverse compared to many other C roles And it difficult to be successful especially like market changes and things accelerate with AI So I really liked the structure at RAMP and I particularly really liked being part of a more technical org, like reporting to the CTO, the technical resources we have, like the very logical problem solving. It was honestly, it drew me. And also I feel like you, putting the growth into the product org kind of unlocks the keys to the kingdom in a lot of ways, right? Oh, yeah, for sure. And I mean, a lot of credit also goes to Kareem, the CTO. Like, he's incredibly open-minded. Like, whether it's crazy stunts or, I mean, we do a lot of crazy experiments. Like, going back to how I don't consider myself a particularly good marketer, but I am a decent problem solver. We solve our problems by just running a bunch of experiments. So I appreciate how he's mad with you. That's a classic good marketing guy to say. Because I think, actually, what does that mean? Some of the best people at marketing are people who do not say they're marketers like Mark Benioff, Richard Branson, Elon Musk. Those guys are not going to say I'm a marketer, but it's like I'm good at getting people to pay attention to these things and solve creative challenges. So I think that's interesting. And then there's clearly some level of buy-in though at that level to like we want to help the company grow. You know, there's some companies where like CTO wants nothing to even do. We're just going to build a product. But it seems like there's a culture of understanding how all these things fit together. 100%. And I think we've – Ramp has done a particularly good job of recognizing early on how important just attention is for a marketing org. It's like, yes, ultimately we need to drive leads and SQLs, but especially as AI commoditizes just a lot of execution, how you get in front of someone and the alpha in how you get in front of someone is really how you capture their attention. It's not like bidding optimization on paid search. It really is capturing attention, which is a lot more qualitative. That's super relevant for right now. I wrote something and I just want to this is not to be corny but I wrote this into our team this morning because it's just like I don't think people who realize how big of a shift is happening in the market because in marketing because of AI, but not for what you might think. It's not because of all the stuff you can build. It's because now like there's no reason to read someone's blog or download a PDF or talk to sales. I can do all that through AI. So marketing
